problem text: As you stroll along an endless avenue, you observe that $6$ out of every $7$ cats on the pavement are succeeded by a dog, whereas $1$ out of every $4$ dogs is succeeded by a cat. What fraction of the creatures on the pavement are dogs?
problem solution: Let $$ c $$ and $$ d $$ be the fractions of cats and dogs on the pavement, respectively. We have $$ c+d = 1 $$. We need to express $$ d $$ in terms of $$ c $$. Observe that of all the cats, $$ \frac{6}{7} $$ are succeeded by a dog. Then, of those dogs, $$ \frac{3}{4} $$ are succeeded by another dog. This pattern continues, so we can express $$ d = \frac{6}{7}c + \frac{6}{7} \cdot \frac{3}{4}c + \frac{6}{7} \cdot \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^2 c + \dots = \frac{6c}{7} \displaystyle \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^k = \frac{24c}{7} $$. Plugging this into our original equation, $$ \frac{31c}{7} = 1 $$, hence $$ c = \frac{7}{31} $$. Therefore, $$ d = \frac{24}{31} $$. Alternatively, using a Markov chain to model this situation, where state $$ 1 $$ is dog and state $$ 2 $$ is cat, the Markov chain is $$\begin{bmatrix} \frac{3}{4} & \frac{1}{4} \ \frac{6}{7} & \frac{1}{7} \end{bmatrix}$$ The steady state of this Markov chain gives the same result as above.
problem hint: Let $c$ and $d$ be the fractions of cats and dogs on the pavement, respectively. Note that of all the cats, $\dfrac{6}{7}$ are succeeded by a dog. Continue using this reasoning.
